title: The Hound of the Baskervilles
author: Arthur Conan Doyle

timeline.span: a few weeks of one autumn (the year is undated in the text; c.1889 by the usual reckoning)

overture: A curse, a moor and a hound: from Baker Street down to Dartmoor, Dr Watson guards the last of the Baskervilles while Sherlock Holmes watches, unseen, from the moor - and the trap closes on the edge of the great Grimpen Mire.
sources: Arthur Conan Doyle, The Hound of the Baskervilles (1902), Project Gutenberg #2852; the real Dartmoor anchors of Doyle's composite moor (Fox Tor Mire, Whiteworks, Grimspound, Princetown) and the Great Western route to Devon.

characters.name: Dr Watson
characters.role: Holmes's friend, sent to Dartmoor to guard Sir Henry and watch the moor.

characters.name: Sir Henry Baskerville
characters.role: The heir home from Canada to claim the Hall - and the quarry the Hound is set on.

characters.name: Sherlock Holmes
characters.role: The detective, who only seems to stay in London while he watches the moor unseen.

locations.name: 221B Baker Street
locations.note: Holmes's rooms in Marylebone; the case opens here.
locations.story: Where the case begins. Dr Mortimer lays before Holmes a walking-stick, an old manuscript of the Baskerville curse, and the fresh death of Sir Charles Baskerville on his own moor - beside the print of a gigantic hound.
locations.quote: Mr. Holmes, they were the footprints of a gigantic hound!
locations.imageBlank: No honest period view of Baker Street surfaced - only modern tube photographs and unrelated portraits; the London rooms keep a logged blank.

locations.name: the moor-edge station
locations.note: Unnamed in the text; placed at Yelverton, the Great Western junction where the Princetown branch left the main line - the logical alighting point for a Hall set near Princetown. The moor's composite geography means no single station is certain.
locations.story: Where Watson and Sir Henry leave the train and the soft country ends. A wagonette waits to carry them up onto the moor, and armed soldiers watch the roads for an escaped convict.

image.caption: Mist lifting off the open moor - the brooding Dartmoor the train delivers them to. Edmund Morison Wimperis, 1896.
image.credit: Edmund Morison Wimperis, 1896 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Baskerville Hall
locations.note: Doyle's invented ancestral house; his setting-cues (the mire in view, a walk to Merripit, Princetown gaol near) place it on the high southern moor. The real seed of the tale is the Cabell legend at Brook Manor, Buckfastleigh, but the mapped setting sits here, near Princetown.
locations.story: The grim old pile at the heart of the story: a heavy hall of grey stone ringed by the brooding moor, where the last Baskerville comes home to a curse and, at night, the long low cry the moor-folk call the Hound.
locations.quote: a great, black beast, shaped like a hound, yet larger than any hound that ever mortal eye has rested upon.

image.caption: A gaunt gothic manor of the period, standing for Doyle's invented Baskerville Hall - Newlands Manor, photographed by Francis Frith, c.1900.
image.credit: Francis Frith, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Grimpen
locations.note: Doyle's fictional moor hamlet - Mortimer's practice, the post office, the telegraph; the name echoes real Grimspound. Placed on the moor a little north of the mire.
locations.story: The scatter of moor cottages that serves the Hall - the doctor's house, the little post office through which a crucial telegram is traced, and the folk who know the moor and fear it.

image.caption: The moor near Princetown at dusk - the kind of country the scattered hamlet of Grimpen sits in.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1903 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Merripit House
locations.note: Stapleton's house 'on the very edge' of the mire; the usual real-world seed is Whiteworks, the ruined tin-mining settlement on the northern lip of Fox Tor Mire - an isolated dwelling against the bog.
locations.story: The naturalist Stapleton's lonely house against the mire - and the trap at the centre of the plot. From here the Hound is loosed; here Sir Henry is asked to dine and sent home alone across the dark moor.
locations.imageBlank: Stapleton's house is Doyle's invention, a lonely dwelling on the mire's edge; no specific period view exists, so a logged blank.

locations.name: the great Grimpen Mire
locations.note: Universally identified with Fox Tor Mire (Foxtor Mires), the great blanket bog south of Princetown - the moor's most notorious mire and the obvious model for the bog that swallows pony and man.
locations.story: The deadly green bog that only Stapleton knows how to cross, its safe path marked by wands. It swallows the moor ponies whole - and, in the end, takes the murderer himself, and keeps him.
locations.quote: It's a bad place, the great Grimpen Mire.

image.caption: A Dartmoor mire (Taw Marsh) - the treacherous bog country of the moor; Doyle's Grimpen Mire is Fox Tor Mire, a little to the south.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1903 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: the stone huts
locations.note: Holmes's hideout is a real Bronze-Age hut circle; Grimspound, the celebrated walled hut settlement on the eastern moor, is the standard exemplar and gives a defensible point.
locations.story: The grey Neolithic huts of the ancient moor-dwellers - and the secret residence of Sherlock Holmes, who never went back to London at all, but watched the whole affair unseen from the heart of the moor.
locations.quote: It is murder, Watson-refined, cold-blooded, deliberate murder.

image.caption: The Bronze-Age hut circles at Grimspound - the real 'stone huts' of the moor that Holmes's hideout is drawn from.
image.credit: From S. Baring-Gould, 'A Book of Dartmoor', 1907 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Coombe Tracey
locations.note: Doyle's fictional market town where Laura Lyons lives, a drive off the moor; near-universally linked to Bovey Tracey on the moor's eastern edge (the shared '-Tracey', the right distance and character).
locations.story: The little town off the moor where Laura Lyons lives - the woman who was lured into writing to Sir Charles on the night he died, and whose story unlocks the plot.
locations.imageBlank: Doyle's fictional market town, off the moor; no honest period view came to hand, so a logged blank.

locations.name: Dartmoor Prison, Princetown
locations.note: The real convict prison (HMP Dartmoor) at Princetown, from which the murderer Selden has escaped; its lights and the warders' searches run through the plot.
locations.story: The great prison on the moor. From it the murderer Selden has escaped, and is hiding among the tors - a second hunted thing on the moor, and the cause of a death mistaken for another.

image.caption: The gate of Dartmoor Prison at Princetown, c.1900, with a convict work-gang - the prison the murderer Selden has escaped from.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Black Tor
locations.note: Doyle names Black Tor as the tor where the watching figure is silhouetted against the moon; there are several real Black Tors on Dartmoor, this one near Princetown a defensible representative.
locations.story: The granite tor where, one moonlit night, Watson sees a tall, thin figure standing motionless against the sky - the mysterious man on the moor, who is not the enemy at all.

image.caption: A weathered granite tor of the moor (Vixen Tor) - the kind of tor Watson sees the mysterious figure stand upon.
image.credit: From S. Baring-Gould, 'A Book of Dartmoor', 1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

movements.note: Holmes sends Watson to guard Sir Henry; they take the ten-thirty from Paddington west into Devon.
movements.routeNote: Doyle names 'the ten-thirty train from Paddington'; the Great Western main line west through Exeter to the edge of Dartmoor.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, ch. 6; the GWR West-Country main line.

movements.note: A wagonette carries them up off the road and onto the open moor to Baskerville Hall.
movements.routeNote: Up from the moor-edge station onto the high moor.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, ch. 6.

movements.note: Holmes, far from staying in London, slips secretly down to Dartmoor and takes up hidden residence in a stone hut on the moor.
movements.routeNote: The same Great Western line west; Holmes's secret journey to the moor, revealed only later.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, chs. 6–11.

movements.note: Watson drives off the moor to Coombe Tracey to question Laura Lyons, who wrote to Sir Charles the night he died.
movements.routeNote: East off the moor to the little town.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, ch. 11.

movements.note: Tracking the mysterious man of the moor to his hut, Watson finds not the enemy - but Holmes himself.
movements.routeNote: Back across the moor to the hut circles.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, ch. 11.

movements.note: That night Sir Henry dines at Merripit House and sets off alone across the dark moor toward home - the bait in Holmes's trap.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, ch. 14.

movements.note: Holmes and Watson cross the moor to lie in wait in the fog on the path below Merripit House.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, ch. 14.

movements.note: In the morning they trace the murderer's flight to the edge of the great Grimpen Mire.
movements.routeSource: The Hound of the Baskervilles, ch. 14.

story.title: The footprints of a gigantic hound
story.narration: The case opens at 221B Baker Street. Dr Mortimer brings Holmes an old manuscript - the curse of a spectral hound that has hunted the Baskervilles since one wicked Hugo was struck down on the moor - and the fresh news that Sir Charles has been found dead there, beside the print of a gigantic hound.

Sir Henry Baskerville, the last of the line, arrives from Canada to claim the Hall. Already he is being watched in London - shadowed by a stranger, and warned in an anonymous note to keep away from the moor.

Holmes, pleading business in town, sends Watson down to guard him. They take the train west from Paddington and step out, at last, at a small moor-edge station where soldiers watch the road for an escaped convict.
A wagonette carries them up onto the open moor to Baskerville Hall - a grim grey pile ringed by the brooding waste, the lights of Princetown gaol winking far off across it.

story.title: Baskerville Hall
story.narration: The Hall broods under its curse. A murderer, Selden, is loose among the tors; a light burns nightly on the moor; and out of the dark comes a long, low moan the country-folk call the cry of the Hound.

story.title: The naturalist Stapleton
story.narration: The naturalist Stapleton, of Merripit House, calls at the Hall and befriends Watson - and warns him off the great Grimpen Mire, the green, treacherous bog nearby that swallows moor-pony and man alike, crossable only by a secret path he alone seems to know.
But Holmes never stayed in London at all.

He has slipped secretly down to Dartmoor and taken up residence in one of the ancient stone huts on the moor, watching the whole affair unseen - the mysterious second figure Watson keeps glimpsing against the sky.
Watson, meanwhile, patiently gathers the threads.
He drives off the moor to Coombe Tracey to question Laura Lyons, who was tricked into asking Sir Charles to meet her the very night he died.
Then, tracking the mysterious man of the moor to his hut, Watson finds - not the enemy - but Holmes himself, who has solved it: Stapleton is a hidden Baskerville, and the Hound is his weapon.

story.title: 'Refined, cold-blooded murder'
story.narration: 'It is murder, Watson-refined, cold-blooded, deliberate murder,' says Holmes from the ancient hut. Stapleton means to kill Sir Henry with a real hound, painted with phosphorus to seem a demon, and inherit the Baskerville estate. The trap is nearly ready.
So they bait it with the one life the killer wants. Sir Henry is to dine at Merripit House and walk home alone across the moor.
He sets off into a rising fog, over the dark moor-path, exactly as Stapleton has planned - and does not know that other men are waiting.
Holmes and Watson lie hidden in the fog by the path, revolvers ready, as the fog thickens and Sir Henry's footsteps come on.

story.title: The Hound
story.narration: Out of the fog bursts the Hound - an enormous black creature, its muzzle and hackles flickering with cold fire. Holmes and Watson empty their revolvers into it and it falls dead. It was flesh and blood after all; Sir Henry is saved.
Stapleton flees for his secret path across the mire in the dark.

story.title: The mire keeps its secret
story.narration: But the fog has hidden the guiding wands, and the bog he knew so well takes him at last. 'It's a bad place, the great Grimpen Mire.' Somewhere out in it the murderer lies; and the curse of the Baskervilles is ended.
